title: Performance
description: Recomposition optimization, memory leaks, profiling, Macrobenchmark, Compose compiler metrics, and APK size
lesson:
Compose Recomposition — The Key to Performance

Compose is fast by default, but understanding recomposition is essential to keeping it fast. Recomposition is the process of re-executing composable functions when their inputs change. The Compose runtime tracks which State objects each composable reads and only re-executes the composables that depend on changed state.

Stability & Skippability

Compose skips recomposition when all parameters are stable and unchanged. A type is stable if Compose can verify it hasn't changed between recompositions. The Compose compiler infers stability automatically for simple types but needs help with complex ones:

Stable Types (Compose can skip): ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Primitives Int, Float, Boolean, String data class All properties must also be stable @Immutable Promise: will NEVER change after creation @Stable Promise: changes are observable via Compose State ImmutableList kotlinx.collections.immutable enum class Finite set of instances, inherently stable Function types Lambdas — stable if they don't capture unstable vars Unstable Types (Compose ALWAYS recomposes): ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ List, Set, Map Interfaces — could be mutable at runtime Regular class No equals/hashCode guarantee var properties Mutable = unstable External types Classes from other modules (unless annotated) Generics with e.g., Result<T> where T is unstable unstable bounds
// ❌ Unstable — List could be mutable data class UiState( val items: List<Item>, // unstable! val isLoading: Boolean ) // ✅ Stable — @Immutable + ImmutableList @Immutable data class UiState( val items: ImmutableList<Item>, // stable val isLoading: Boolean )

@Stable vs @Immutable — Know the Difference

Both annotations are contracts you make with the Compose compiler. Violating them causes subtle bugs (stale UI, missed updates).

// @Immutable: "I promise this object NEVER changes after construction" // Use for: data transfer objects, UI models, configuration @Immutable data class Theme(val primary: Color, val background: Color) // @Stable: "I promise changes are tracked via Compose State" // Use for: observable state holders with mutableStateOf properties @Stable class CounterState { var count by mutableStateOf(0) // changes ARE observable } // ❌ WRONG: @Stable with non-observable mutation @Stable class BadState { var count = 0 // NOT mutableStateOf — Compose won't see changes! }
@Immutable vs @Stable rule of thumb:
- Use @Immutable when the object is truly read-only after creation (all val properties, no mutable collections)
- Use @Stable when the object has mutable state tracked by mutableStateOf
- Never annotate a class as @Immutable if it holds a var or a mutable collection — the compiler trusts you and will skip recomposition even when data has changed

Strong Skipping Mode (Compose Compiler 1.5.4+)

Strong skipping mode changes how Compose handles unstable parameters and lambdas:

// Enable in build.gradle.kts (default in Compose Compiler 2.0+) composeCompiler { enableStrongSkippingMode = true } // With strong skipping: // - Unstable params are compared by instance equality (===) // instead of forcing recomposition // - Lambdas are automatically remembered by the compiler // - Most composables become skippable without @Stable/@Immutable // Before strong skipping: this always recomposes (List is unstable) @Composable fun ItemList(items: List<String>) { ... } // With strong skipping: skips if same List instance (===) // Still recomposes if you create a new List each time
Strong skipping does NOT replace good practices. You still benefit from @Immutable, ImmutableList, and derivedStateOf. Strong skipping is a safety net — it makes accidental instability less costly, but explicit stability contracts are still faster because they use .equals() comparison (structural) rather than === (referential).

derivedStateOf — Avoid Redundant Recomposition

derivedStateOf creates a derived state that only triggers recomposition when the computed result changes, not when the input changes. It is the Compose equivalent of a computed property with memoization.

// Without derivedStateOf: recomposes on EVERY scroll position change val showButton = listState.firstVisibleItemIndex > 0 // ❌ triggers on every pixel // With derivedStateOf: only recomposes when the RESULT changes (false→true, true→false) val showButton by remember { derivedStateOf { listState.firstVisibleItemIndex > 0 } // ✅ } // Another example: filtering a large list val filteredItems by remember(allItems) { derivedStateOf { allItems.filter { it.category == selectedCategory } // ✅ only recomposes when filter result changes } } // ❌ DON'T use derivedStateOf when input and output change at the same rate val doubled by remember { derivedStateOf { count * 2 } } // ❌ pointless — changes every time count changes
When to use derivedStateOf: Only when the input state changes more frequently than the derived output. Classic examples: scroll-dependent visibility flags, search filtering, threshold-based UI changes. If input and output always change together, derivedStateOf adds overhead with no benefit.

key in LazyColumn — Stable Item Identity

Without keys, LazyColumn identifies items by their index position. This means inserting or removing items causes all subsequent items to recompose because their position changed. Providing a stable key lets Compose track items across reorderings.

// ❌ Without key: items are identified by position // Inserting at top = ALL items recompose (positions shifted) LazyColumn { items(users) { user -> UserCard(user) } } // ✅ With key: items are identified by stable ID // Inserting at top = only new item composes, others are reused LazyColumn { items(users, key = { it.id }) { user -> UserCard(user) } } // Keys also preserve state across reorderings: LazyColumn { items(users, key = { it.id }) { user -> var expanded by rememberSaveable { mutableStateOf(false) } UserCard(user, expanded) // expanded state follows the item, not the position } } // contentType improves recycling performance in mixed lists LazyColumn { items(feed, key = { it.id }) { item -> when (item) { is Header -> HeaderCard(item) is Post -> PostCard(item) } } }

Baseline Profiles

Baseline Profiles are lists of critical classes and methods that should be AOT-compiled at install time. Without them, the first launch uses interpretation and JIT — slow. With them, hot code paths are native code before the app even opens.

How Baseline Profiles Work Under the Hood

Without Baseline Profile (cold start): ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Install → DEX stored as-is 1st Launch → Interpreter runs bytecode (SLOW) → JIT compiles hot methods over time → Profile collected after hours of use Background → ART compiles profile → native code Next launch → Faster (but user already suffered) With Baseline Profile (cold start): ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Install → ART reads baseline-prof.txt → AOT-compiles listed methods immediately 1st Launch → Critical paths are ALREADY native code → Fast from the very first open → JIT still improves unlisted methods ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Generating Baseline Profiles

// Step 1: Add the Baseline Profile Gradle plugin // build.gradle.kts (app module) plugins { id("androidx.baselineprofile") } dependencies { baselineProfile(project(":baselineprofile")) } // Step 2: Create a :baselineprofile module with the generator @RunWith(AndroidJUnit4::class) class BaselineProfileGenerator { @get:Rule val rule = BaselineProfileRule() @Test fun generateProfile() = rule.collect( packageName = "com.example.app" ) { // Exercise critical user journeys startActivityAndWait() device.findObject(By.text("Search")).click() device.wait(Until.hasObject(By.res("results")), 5000) // Navigate to detail screen device.findObject(By.res("item_0")).click() device.waitForIdle() } // Output: baseline-prof.txt placed in app/src/main/ } // Step 3: Generate with Gradle // ./gradlew :app:generateBaselineProfile

Startup Profiles vs Baseline Profiles

Baseline Profile: Lists methods to AOT-compile. Improves runtime speed of those methods.

Startup Profile (API 34+): Additionally optimizes DEX layout — rearranges classes in the DEX file so that startup classes are grouped together on the same memory pages. This reduces page faults during cold start. The BaselineProfileRule generates both automatically when targeting API 34+.
Measurable impact:
- Cold startup: up to 30% faster
- Time to first frame: significantly reduced
- Scroll jank: reduced on first use
- DEX file page faults: reduced with startup profiles
Google Play auto-generates cloud profiles after enough installs, but shipping your own gives every user the benefit from day one.

Macrobenchmark

Macrobenchmark tests run as a separate instrumentation APK against your full app. They measure real-world performance on a real device, not microbenchmarks of individual functions.

Startup Benchmark

// Measures TTID (Time To Initial Display) and TTFD (Time To Full Display) @RunWith(AndroidJUnit4::class) class StartupBenchmark { @get:Rule val benchmarkRule = MacrobenchmarkRule() @Test fun startupCold() = benchmarkRule.measureRepeated( packageName = "com.example.app", metrics = listOf(StartupTimingMetric()), iterations = 5, startupMode = StartupMode.COLD, // process killed between iterations compilationMode = CompilationMode.DEFAULT // use baseline profile ) { pressHome() startActivityAndWait() } // Test WITHOUT baseline profile to measure the improvement @Test fun startupColdNoProfile() = benchmarkRule.measureRepeated( packageName = "com.example.app", metrics = listOf(StartupTimingMetric()), iterations = 5, startupMode = StartupMode.COLD, compilationMode = CompilationMode.None() // no AOT, pure JIT ) { pressHome() startActivityAndWait() } }

Scroll Jank Benchmark

// Measures frame timing — detects janky frames @Test fun scrollPerformance() = benchmarkRule.measureRepeated( packageName = "com.example.app", metrics = listOf(FrameTimingMetric()), iterations = 5, startupMode = StartupMode.WARM ) { startActivityAndWait() val list = device.findObject(By.res("item_list")) list.setGestureMargin(device.displayWidth / 5) list.fling(Direction.DOWN) device.waitForIdle() } // Custom trace section benchmark @Test fun measureImageLoading() = benchmarkRule.measureRepeated( packageName = "com.example.app", metrics = listOf(TraceSectionMetric("ImageDecode")), // matches trace("ImageDecode") in app code iterations = 5 ) { startActivityAndWait() device.findObject(By.res("gallery")).click() device.waitForIdle() }
Startup Modes: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ COLD Process killed → fork from Zygote → Application.onCreate() → Activity.onCreate() → first frame Most expensive. Tests full startup path. WARM Process alive, Activity destroyed → Activity.onCreate() → first frame. No Application init. HOT Activity paused → resumed. Cheapest. Tests resume speed. Compilation Modes: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ None() Pure interpreter + JIT (worst case) Partial() Use baseline profile only DEFAULT System default compilation Full() Fully AOT (best case, unrealistic) ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Metrics available:
- StartupTimingMetric — TTID (first frame) and TTFD (reportFullyDrawn())
- FrameTimingMetric — frame duration, jank count, jank percentage
- TraceSectionMetric — duration of custom android.os.Trace sections in your code
- PowerMetric — battery drain during the test
- MemoryUsageMetric — RSS, PSS, heap size snapshots
Run benchmarks on a physical device with animations disabled and consistent thermal state for reliable results.

R8 / ProGuard — Code Shrinking & Optimization

R8 is Google's replacement for ProGuard (default since AGP 3.4). It performs three functions in a single pass, operating directly on DEX bytecode instead of Java bytecode:

R8 Pipeline: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 1. SHRINKING Remove unused classes, methods, fields Tree-shaking from entry points Typical result: 20-40% code reduction 2. OPTIMIZATION Inline methods, simplify code, remove dead branches Merge classes, devirtualize calls Improves runtime performance 3. OBFUSCATION Rename classes/methods to a, b, c... Reduces DEX size + makes reverse engineering harder Produces mapping.txt for stack trace deobfuscation ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Configuration

// build.gradle.kts — enable R8 android { buildTypes { release { isMinifyEnabled = true // enable R8 shrinking + optimization + obfuscation isShrinkResources = true // remove unused resources (drawables, layouts, etc.) proguardFiles( getDefaultProguardFile("proguard-android-optimize.txt"), "proguard-rules.pro" ) } } }

Keep Rules — Preserving Code from Shrinking

// proguard-rules.pro // Keep entire class hierarchy (for reflection / serialization) -keep class com.example.api.models.** { *; } // Keep only annotated fields (more precise) -keepclassmembers class * { @com.google.gson.annotations.SerializedName <fields>; } // Keep class name but allow member shrinking -keepnames class com.example.MyClass // Keep all classes implementing an interface -keep class * implements com.example.Plugin { *; } // Keep for JNI (native methods must match exactly) -keepclasseswithmembernames class * { native <methods>; } // Suppress warnings for known libraries -dontwarn okhttp3.** // Keep rules granularity: // -keep = keep class AND members // -keepclassmembers = keep members only (class can be removed if unused) // -keepnames = allow shrinking, prevent obfuscation // -keepclassmembernames = keep member names only

mapping.txt — Deobfuscating Stack Traces

// R8 produces mapping.txt at: // app/build/outputs/mapping/release/mapping.txt // Obfuscated crash: "at a.b.c.d(Unknown Source:12)" // mapping.txt maps: a.b.c.d → com.example.UserRepo.fetchUser // Deobfuscate with retrace tool: // retrace mapping.txt stacktrace.txt // Google Play Console / Firebase Crashlytics automatically // deobfuscate if you upload mapping.txt with each release. // AGP can auto-upload: firebaseCrashlytics { mappingFileUploadEnabled = true // auto-uploads on build }
Common R8 pitfalls:
- Reflection-based libraries (Gson, Retrofit) need keep rules — R8 removes "unused" code that's actually accessed via reflection
- kotlinx.serialization avoids this problem (compile-time code gen, no reflection)
- Always test your release build thoroughly — R8 can break code that uses reflection
- mapping.txt is unique per build — you must keep the exact mapping.txt for each release version to deobfuscate crash reports
- R8 full mode (android.enableR8.fullMode=true) is more aggressive — additional optimizations but may need more keep rules

Memory Management — Leaks, Allocations, WeakReference

Android Memory Model

Android Process Memory: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ HEAP (dalvik + native) Objects, bitmaps, buffers GC pauses when heap is full → jank Each app has a heap limit (128-512MB typically) STACK Local variables, method call frames StackOverflowError if too deep (recursion) Memory pressure responses: onTrimMemory(TRIM_MEMORY_RUNNING_LOW) → free caches onTrimMemory(TRIM_MEMORY_UI_HIDDEN) → release UI resources onTrimMemory(TRIM_MEMORY_COMPLETE) → about to be killed ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Detecting Leaks with LeakCanary

// Just add the dependency — it's automatic! dependencies { debugImplementation("com.squareup.leakcanary:leakcanary-android:2.14") } // LeakCanary automatically: // 1. Watches destroyed Activities, Fragments, ViewModels, Services // 2. Waits 5 seconds, then checks if they were garbage collected // 3. If retained: dumps heap and finds the reference chain // 4. Shows a notification with the leak trace // Custom watchers for your own objects class MyFragment : Fragment() { override fun onDestroyView() { super.onDestroyView() AppWatcher.objectWatcher.expectWeaklyReachable( myHeavyObject, "MyFragment.myHeavyObject" ) } } // LeakCanary leak trace output (example): // ┬─── // │ GC Root: System class // ├─ Analytics.callback // │ ↓ Analytics.callback // ├─ MainActivity$1 (anonymous inner class) // │ ↓ MainActivity$1.this$0 // ╰→ MainActivity (LEAKING)

Common Leak Patterns

// ❌ LEAK: Singleton holds Activity reference object Analytics { var callback: (() -> Unit)? = null // holds Activity forever! } // ✅ FIX: Use WeakReference object Analytics { private var callbackRef: WeakReference<(() -> Unit)>? = null fun setCallback(cb: () -> Unit) { callbackRef = WeakReference(cb) } fun fire() { callbackRef?.get()?.invoke() } // null if GC'd } // ❌ LEAK: Inner class holds outer Activity class MyActivity : AppCompatActivity() { val handler = object : Handler(Looper.getMainLooper()) { override fun handleMessage(msg: Message) { updateUI() // implicit reference to MyActivity } } } // ✅ FIX: Static inner class + WeakReference class MyHandler(activity: MyActivity) : Handler(Looper.getMainLooper()) { private val activityRef = WeakReference(activity) override fun handleMessage(msg: Message) { activityRef.get()?.updateUI() } } // ❌ LEAK: Coroutine scope outlives lifecycle class MyActivity : AppCompatActivity() { fun fetchData() { GlobalScope.launch { // lives forever — captures 'this' val data = api.getData() updateUI(data) // Activity reference held until coroutine completes } } } // ✅ FIX: Use lifecycleScope class MyActivity : AppCompatActivity() { fun fetchData() { lifecycleScope.launch { // cancelled when Activity is destroyed val data = api.getData() updateUI(data) } } }

WeakReference vs SoftReference

WeakReference: Collected at the next GC cycle regardless of memory pressure. Use for: preventing leaks (callbacks, listeners, caches where you don't mind re-fetching).

SoftReference: Collected only when memory is low. Use for: memory-sensitive caches (bitmaps, computed results) — kept as long as there is available heap. In practice, prefer LruCache over SoftReference for caching because LruCache gives you explicit size control.

Avoiding Allocations in Hot Paths

// ❌ Allocating in draw/measure/scroll = GC pressure = jank override fun onDraw(canvas: Canvas) { val paint = Paint() // new object EVERY frame at 60fps = 60 objects/sec! val rect = RectF(0f, 0f, width.toFloat(), height.toFloat()) // another allocation! canvas.drawRect(rect, paint) } // ✅ Pre-allocate and reuse private val paint = Paint().apply { color = Color.RED style = Paint.Style.FILL } private val rect = RectF() override fun onDraw(canvas: Canvas) { rect.set(0f, 0f, width.toFloat(), height.toFloat()) canvas.drawRect(rect, paint) // zero allocations per frame } // ❌ String concatenation in a loop for (item in largeList) { log("Processing: " + item.name) // creates new String each iteration } // ✅ StringBuilder or structured logging val sb = StringBuilder() for (item in largeList) { sb.clear().append("Processing: ").append(item.name) log(sb.toString()) } // ❌ Autoboxing in collections val ids = mutableListOf<Int>() // autoboxes every Int → Integer object // ✅ Use Android sparse collections or IntArray val ids = IntArray(size) // primitive array, no boxing val map = SparseIntArray() // int→int with no boxing

onTrimMemory — Responding to Memory Pressure

// Implement ComponentCallbacks2 in your Application class class App : Application() { override fun onTrimMemory(level: Int) { super.onTrimMemory(level) when (level) { TRIM_MEMORY_RUNNING_LOW -> { // App visible but system is low on memory imageCache.evictAll() } TRIM_MEMORY_UI_HIDDEN -> { // App went to background — release UI resources releaseHeavyBitmaps() } TRIM_MEMORY_COMPLETE -> { // Process will be killed soon — release everything possible imageCache.evictAll() dataCache.clear() } } } }

App Startup Optimization

Cold startup time directly impacts user retention. Studies show that every 100ms of delay reduces conversions. The startup path has several distinct phases you can optimize:

Cold Start Timeline: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Process Fork (Zygote) │ ~50ms — not optimizable ▼ ContentProvider.onCreate() — ALL providers run here │ Each library's ContentProvider adds 2-10ms │ Firebase, WorkManager, Lifecycle all use them ▼ Application.onCreate() │ DI initialization (Dagger/Hilt component creation) │ SDK initialization (analytics, crash reporting) ▼ Activity.onCreate() │ Layout inflation / first composition │ ▼ │ TTID — Time To Initial Display (first frame drawn) │ ▼ │ Data loading (network, database) │ ▼ │ TTFD — Time To Full Display (reportFullyDrawn()) ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

App Startup Library (Jetpack)

// Problem: ContentProviders initialize eagerly at app start // Libraries like WorkManager, Firebase use ContentProviders // 10 libraries = 10 ContentProviders = 20-100ms added // Solution: App Startup library merges them into ONE ContentProvider class MyInitializer : Initializer<MyLibrary> { override fun create(context: Context): MyLibrary { return MyLibrary.init(context) } override fun dependencies(): List<Class<out Initializer<*>>> { // Declare dependencies — App Startup resolves order return listOf(WorkManagerInitializer::class.java) } } // Disable automatic WorkManager init (it uses its own ContentProvider) // AndroidManifest.xml: // <provider android:name="androidx.startup.InitializationProvider" // android:authorities="..."> // <meta-data android:name="androidx.work.WorkManagerInitializer" // android:value="androidx.startup" /> // </provider> // Lazy initialization — don't load until actually needed class App : Application() { val analytics by lazy { Analytics.init(this) } // deferred to first use val imageLoader by lazy { ImageLoader.Builder(this).build() } override fun onCreate() { super.onCreate() // Only initialize what is truly needed before first frame CrashReporter.init(this) // must be first to catch startup crashes // Everything else: lazy or background thread } }

reportFullyDrawn — Measuring Real Startup

// TTID = when the first frame is drawn (automatic) // TTFD = when the app is actually usable (you must signal this!) class MainActivity : AppCompatActivity() { override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) { super.onCreate(savedInstanceState) setContent { val state by viewModel.uiState.collectAsState() if (state.isReady) { LaunchedEffect(Unit) { reportFullyDrawn() // signal to system: app is ready } } MainScreen(state) } } } // Macrobenchmark's StartupTimingMetric captures both: // - timeToInitialDisplayMs (TTID) // - timeToFullDisplayMs (TTFD) — requires reportFullyDrawn() // Play Vitals also tracks TTFD to show real user startup times

SplashScreen API (API 31+)

// Modern splash screen (replaces old theme hack) // Works on API 23+ via the androidx.core SplashScreen compat library class MainActivity : AppCompatActivity() { override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) { val splashScreen = installSplashScreen() // Keep splash visible while loading initial data splashScreen.setKeepOnScreenCondition { viewModel.isLoading.value // true = keep showing splash } // Custom exit animation splashScreen.setOnExitAnimationListener { provider -> provider.view.animate() .alpha(0f) .setDuration(300) .withEndAction { provider.remove() } .start() } super.onCreate(savedInstanceState) } } // Key: installSplashScreen() MUST be called before super.onCreate() // The splash screen is backed by the windowBackground theme attribute // Customize in themes.xml: // <style name="Theme.App.Starting" parent="Theme.SplashScreen"> // <item name="windowSplashScreenBackground">@color/bg</item> // <item name="windowSplashScreenAnimatedIcon">@drawable/icon</item> // <item name="postSplashScreenTheme">@style/Theme.App</item> // </style>

Compose Compiler Reports

The Compose compiler can report which classes are stable/unstable and which composables are skippable. This is the single best tool for diagnosing unnecessary recomposition.

// Enable in build.gradle.kts composeCompiler { reportsDestination = layout.buildDirectory.dir("compose_metrics") metricsDestination = layout.buildDirectory.dir("compose_metrics") } // Alternative (older syntax): kotlinOptions { freeCompilerArgs += listOf( "-P", "plugin:androidx.compose.compiler.plugins.kotlin:reportsDestination=" + project.buildDir.absolutePath + "/compose_metrics" ) } // Run: ./gradlew :app:assembleRelease // Check: app/build/compose_metrics/ // Output: app_release-composables.txt restartable skippable scheme("[...]") fun UserCard( stable user: User stable onSelect: Function1<String, Unit> ) // "skippable" = good! Compose can skip recomposition // If you see "restartable" without "skippable" = problem // Output: app_release-classes.txt stable class User { stable val id: Int stable val name: String } unstable class UiState { unstable val items: List<Item> // ← this is why composables can't skip stable val isLoading: Boolean }

APK Size Reduction

APK size affects download conversion rates, install success on low-storage devices, and update speed. Every MB matters.

APK Size Reduction Strategies: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ CODE R8 shrinking minifyEnabled = true ~20-40% R8 obfuscation Shorter names = smaller DEX Remove unused deps Audit with dependency analysis RESOURCES Resource shrinking shrinkResources = true ~5-15% WebP conversion PNGs → WebP ~25-35% Vector drawables Replace raster where possible resConfigs Keep only needed locales/densities PACKAGING Android App Bundle AAB → split APKs per device ~15-20% Dynamic delivery Download features on demand Asset packs Large assets loaded at runtime ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
// Limit included language/density configs android { defaultConfig { resourceConfigurations += listOf("en", "es", "fr") // only these locales ndk { abiFilters += listOf("arm64-v8a", "armeabi-v7a") } // drop x86 } } // Analyze APK contents with Android Studio: // Build → Analyze APK → see size breakdown by component // or: bundletool dump resources --bundle=app.aab

questions.title: Causes of Recomposition
questions.description: Which causes unnecessary recompositions in Compose?
options: Reading a State value inside a composable
Passing an unstable type (class without equals/hashCode) as a parameter
Using remember{} to cache computations
Using LazyColumn with a key parameter
questions.explanation: Compose skips recomposition when parameters are stable and unchanged. Unstable types (without proper equals/hashCode) force recomposition every time because Compose can't tell if they changed. Use data class or annotate with @Stable/@Immutable.
hints: How does Compose decide whether to skip recomposition?

questions.title: Unstable Lambda Capture
questions.description: This recomposes ItemRow every time the parent recomposes. Optimize it.
questions.explanation: Inline lambdas create new instances every recomposition, making them "unstable" parameters. remember(item.id) { { ... } } caches the lambda and only recreates it when item.id changes.
hints: Every recomposition creates new lambda objects

questions.title: Memory Leak
questions.description: This code leaks memory. The Activity is never garbage collected after rotation. Fix it.

checks.message: Clean up the listener in onDestroy

questions.explanation: The anonymous inner class holds a reference to MainActivity. Since the singleton lives forever, the old Activity can't be garbage collected after rotation. Fix: clear the listener in onDestroy().
hints: Singleton objects live for the entire app process

questions.title: Baseline Profiles
questions.description: What are Baseline Profiles and how do they improve performance?
questions.answer: Baseline Profiles are lists of critical classes and methods that should be AOT-compiled during installation. They improve cold startup time (up to 30%) by ensuring hot code paths are native code when the app launches.
questions.explanation: Without Baseline Profiles, ART uses interpretation and JIT compilation. Cold starts are slow. Baseline Profiles tell the system which methods to AOT-compile at install time. The Macrobenchmark library generates them automatically.
hints: What if the system knew which methods were important before the first launch?

questions.title: Bitmap Memory Leak
questions.description: This image loading code causes OutOfMemoryError with large images. Fix it to load images efficiently.

checks.message: Downsample the bitmap or use an image loading library

questions.explanation: A 12MP photo is ~48MB in memory as a Bitmap. inJustDecodeBounds = true reads dimensions without loading pixels. inSampleSize downsamples (2 = half size = 1/4 memory). In practice, use Coil or Glide which handle this automatically with caching, lifecycle awareness, and Compose integration.
hints: You can read dimensions without loading the full bitmap

questions.title: R8 vs ProGuard
questions.description: What is the relationship between R8 and ProGuard in modern Android development?
options: They are the same tool with different names
R8 replaced ProGuard as the default — it does the same things (shrink, optimize, obfuscate) but faster, and uses ProGuard rule syntax
ProGuard handles code, R8 handles resources
R8 is only for Kotlin, ProGuard is for Java
questions.explanation: R8 is Google's replacement for ProGuard. It performs the same functions (code shrinking, optimization, obfuscation) but is faster and produces smaller output. It uses the same rule syntax as ProGuard (proguard-rules.pro), making migration seamless. R8 has been the default since AGP 3.4.
hints: R8 is backward-compatible with ProGuard rules

questions.title: Macrobenchmark Startup Modes
questions.description: What is the difference between COLD, WARM, and HOT startup in Macrobenchmark?
options: COLD = first install, WARM = update, HOT = cached
COLD = process killed, app started fresh; WARM = process alive, activity destroyed; HOT = activity paused and resumed
COLD = no network, WARM = Wi-Fi, HOT = 5G
They all measure the same thing with different iteration counts
questions.explanation: COLD: Process is dead — full startup from scratch (Zygote fork → Application → Activity). Most expensive, most realistic for first launch. WARM: Process is alive but Activity was destroyed — no Application.onCreate(). HOT: Activity exists, just brought back to foreground — cheapest. COLD start is the most important to optimize (Baseline Profiles, reducing ContentProvider inits).
hints: Think about what Android recreates at each level

questions.title: Compose Compiler Report
questions.description: What does this Compose compiler report tell you about the composable?
questions.code:
restartable scheme("[...]") fun ItemCard(
  unstable items: List<Item>
  stable title: String
  stable onSelect: Function1<String, Unit>
)
options: ItemCard is fully optimized and will skip recomposition when inputs haven't changed
ItemCard is restartable but NOT skippable because 'items' is unstable (List is not stable by default) — it recomposes every time the parent recomposes even if data hasn't changed
ItemCard will crash at runtime due to the unstable parameter
The report shows that ItemCard uses too much memory
questions.explanation: The report shows restartable but NOT skippable — this is a performance problem. The culprit is unstable items: List<Item>. Kotlin's List is an interface that could be a MutableList at runtime, so Compose can't guarantee it hasn't changed. Fix options: (1) Use @Immutable or @Stable on Item, (2) Use kotlinx.collections.immutable.ImmutableList, (3) Use SnapshotStateList.
hints: A composable needs ALL stable parameters to be 'skippable'
List is unstable because it's an interface — it could be mutable

questions.title: APK Size Reduction
questions.description: Which combination of settings enables both code and resource shrinking for a release build?
options: proguardFiles only
minifyEnabled = true and shrinkResources = true
shrinkResources = true alone (it implies minifyEnabled)
debuggable = false enables all optimizations automatically
questions.explanation: Both must be set explicitly. minifyEnabled = true enables R8 code shrinking (removes unused classes/methods). shrinkResources = true removes unused resources (images, layouts). Important: shrinkResources requires minifyEnabled — it can't work alone because it needs R8 to determine which resources are referenced in code. Together they can reduce APK size by 20-50%.
hints: shrinkResources depends on minifyEnabled — order matters

questions.title: Doze Mode & Battery
questions.description: What happens to your app's background work when the device enters Doze mode?
options: All background work is permanently killed
Network access, jobs, syncs, and alarms are deferred to periodic maintenance windows
Only GPS is disabled, everything else continues
Doze mode only affects apps targeting API 23+
questions.explanation: Doze mode restricts: network access, JobScheduler/WorkManager jobs, syncs, standard alarms, and wake locks. Work is deferred to periodic maintenance windows where the system briefly exits Doze. setExactAndAllowWhileIdle() alarms still fire but are rate-limited. Firebase Cloud Messaging high-priority messages can wake the device. Doze deepens over time — longer idle = longer between maintenance windows.
hints: Doze doesn't kill work — it defers it to save battery

questions.title: Non-Skippable Composable
questions.description: The Compose compiler report shows this composable is NOT skippable. Fix it so Compose can skip recomposition when data hasn't changed.

checks.message: Make the data class stable for Compose

questions.explanation: Two changes make this skippable: (1) @Immutable on UiState tells Compose the class won't change after construction. (2) ImmutableList (from kotlinx-collections-immutable) replaces List — Compose knows it's truly immutable. Regular List is unstable because the interface could be backed by a MutableList. With both changes, the Compose compiler marks ItemList as skippable.
hints: The issue is List<Item> — Compose can't trust it won't mutate

questions.title: 16ms Frame Budget
questions.description: Explain the 16ms frame budget. What happens when a frame takes 20ms? How would you use systrace/Perfetto to identify whether the bottleneck is in measure, layout, draw, or the render thread?
questions.answer: At 60fps, each frame has 16.67ms to complete. If a frame takes 20ms, it misses the VSYNC deadline — the previous frame is displayed for an extra cycle (33ms), causing visible jank. In Perfetto/systrace, look at the 'Choreographer#doFrame' trace section: it shows INPUT, ANIMATION, and TRAVERSAL phases. Within TRAVERSAL, you'll see measure, layout, and draw timing. If 'draw' is fine but the frame is still late, check the RenderThread track — GPU work may be the bottleneck. Common causes: nested layout weight (double measure), heavy onDraw(), or overdraw.
questions.explanation: The VSYNC signal drives frame rendering. At 60fps each frame has ~16ms. The Choreographer schedules work in phases: input handling, animations, then view traversals (measure/layout/draw). Perfetto shows each phase's duration, making it easy to pinpoint the bottleneck. The RenderThread track reveals GPU-side issues. Look for frames where the Choreographer#doFrame slice exceeds 16ms.
hints: Perfetto has separate tracks for the UI thread and RenderThread

learnMore.title: Android Developers — Inspect Performance with Perfetto
